Great experience attending AWS Summit NYC this week and connecting with AWS teams, partners, startups, and technology leaders.
While there were many announcements, a few takeaways
🔹 Amazon Bedrock AgentCore
AWS continues to push the Agentic AI space forward with new capabilities including Web Search on AgentCore, Managed Knowledge Bases, and AgentCore Harness—making it easier to build, ground, and operationalize AI agents.
🔹 Amazon Q
The evolution of Amazon Q reinforces AWS's vision of bringing AI assistance directly into developer, operations, and business workflows.
🔹 AWS DevOps Agent
AI is increasingly becoming part of day-to-day IT operations, helping teams improve operational efficiency and accelerate issue resolution.
🔹 AWS Continuum
One of the more interesting concepts introduced was AWS Continuum, extending AI across the software engineering and security lifecycle with capabilities such as code review, threat modeling, penetration testing, and continuous modernization.
🔹 Release Management
AWS also showcased how AI can help validate releases, identify issues before production deployment, detect regressions earlier, and improve software delivery confidence.
My biggest takeaway:
The conversation is rapidly shifting from AI assistants to AI-powered systems that actively participate across the entire technology lifecycle—from knowledge retrieval and software development to testing, security, operations, and modernization.
Excited to see how organizations leverage these capabilities to accelerate innovation while improving quality, security, and operational efficiency.

Last week I wrote about the four enterprise AI governance failures that made headlines in May.
$500M in a month. Uber's budget gone by April. Microsoft pulling Claude Code licenses. Amazon shutting down its usage leaderboard.
This week I want to be constructive.
Because the lesson from all four incidents is not "AI is too expensive." It is "AI deployed without governance is too expensive."
For retail and CPG leaders planning the next AI deployment, here is the framework that prevents the incidents above:
Before you deploy:
Build the outcome measurement model first. Define what business metric each agent or workflow is expected to move. Forecast accuracy. Waste reduction. Stockout rate. Logistics cost per delivery. If you cannot define the metric before deployment, you cannot govern the spend after.
When you deploy:
Turn on the controls. Google Cloud's Gemini Enterprise platform has native agent governance built in — hard spending caps, role-based model access, real-time telemetry, automated alerts. These tools exist. The $500M enterprise had them available. They simply were not configured. Configuration is not optional.
After you deploy:
Measure outcomes, not consumption. Token count is not a KPI. Leaderboards ranking AI usage destroyed both Uber's budget and Amazon's engineering incentives. The metric you choose determines the behavior you get. Reward what the business needs.
Goldman Sachs projects token demand will grow 24 times by 2030. The governance infrastructure you build in 2026 is the foundation for every agentic deployment that follows.
For grocery operators at 1.6% net margin, that foundation is not optional.
Full governance brief and the three questions every CxO should answer before the next AI deployment — linked in the comments.

🚀 Why Amazon Bedrock is becoming my preferred starting point for Generative AI projects
Over the last few months, I've been exploring different approaches for building AI-powered applications, from simple Proof of Concepts (POCs) to production-ready systems. One service that consistently stands out is Amazon Bedrock.
For many organizations, the biggest challenge isn't choosing an LLM—it's getting from idea to value quickly while maintaining security, governance, and operational simplicity.
Why Bedrock works well for POCs:
✅ No infrastructure to manage
✅ Access to multiple foundation models through a single API
✅ Rapid experimentation without model hosting complexity
✅ Easy integration with existing AWS services
✅ Pay-as-you-go pricing that avoids large upfront investments
A developer can go from an idea to a working prototype in hours instead of spending days setting up GPUs, model serving platforms, networking, and observability.
Why it scales for production workloads:
✅ Enterprise-grade security and IAM integration
✅ Private networking options within AWS environments
✅ Guardrails and responsible AI capabilities
✅ Knowledge Bases and Agent capabilities for GenAI applications
✅ Monitoring, governance, and operational controls expected by enterprises
✅ Ability to switch or evaluate models without major application redesign
What I find particularly valuable is that teams can start small with a POC and continue using the same platform as requirements evolve into production-grade systems. The journey from experimentation to enterprise deployment feels much smoother compared to managing self-hosted model infrastructure.
For organizations already invested in AWS, Bedrock significantly lowers the barrier to adopting Generative AI while allowing teams to focus on business outcomes rather than infrastructure management.
The future of AI development is not just about the models—it's about how quickly and safely teams can build, iterate, and operate AI-powered systems at scale.
#AWS #AmazonBedrock #GenerativeAI #AI #CloudComputing #MachineLearning #DevOps #PlatformEngineering #CloudArchitecture

title_original: AI Business Case Builder: Simplify ROI Calculations for Execs
body_original:
I spent 30 years in enterprise IT.
I spent 30 years in enterprise IT. Most recently as VP of Technology at Fox Corporation — a role I recently stepped away from as the company reshaped its technology organisation around the AI era.
And in those 30 years, I've never seen anything move as fast as generative AI has since ChatGPT launched in late 2022.
But here's what I have seen — the same thing happen over and over again since that moment.
A team would get excited about AI. They'd find a tool, run a pilot, see real results. And then hit a wall.
Not a technical wall. A business case wall.
Suddenly they needed an ROI model. A payback period. An NPV calculation.
A one-pager for the Sr Management and Finance that didn't look like it was built in a weekend.
And because nobody had a template, they'd either spend weeks building one from scratch — or hire a consultant at $10,000+ to do it for them.
I watched smart, capable people get stuck at that exact moment, including myself. Not because they didn't understand the value of what they were building. But because they didn't have the right tool to articulate it.
So I built one.
The AI Business Case Builder is a 5-tab Excel workbook that takes you from blank page to exec-ready ROI model in under 30 minutes. Labor savings, error reduction, revenue uplift — all risk-adjusted and confidence-weighted. Three-year model. Auto-generated executive briefing. No finance background required.
It's everything I wish had existed every time I had to justify a technology investment to people that needed numbers, not enthusiasm.
$47. One-time download. Available now on Gumroad — link in the comments.
If you know someone trying to get AI approved inside their organization, tag them. This might save them a few weeks.

body_clean:
I spent 30 years in enterprise IT.
I spent 30 years in enterprise IT. Most recently as VP of Technology at Fox Corporation — a role I recently stepped away from as the company reshaped its technology organisation around the AI era.
And in those 30 years, I've never seen anything move as fast as generative AI has since ChatGPT launched in late 2022.
But here's what I have seen — the same thing happen over and over again since that moment.
A team would get excited about AI. They'd find a tool, run a pilot, see real results. And then hit a wall.
Not a technical wall. A business case wall.
Suddenly they needed an ROI model. A payback period. An NPV calculation.
A one-pager for the Sr Management and Finance that didn't look like it was built in a weekend.
And because nobody had a template, they'd either spend weeks building one from scratch — or hire a consultant at $10,000+ to do it for them.
I watched smart, capable people get stuck at that exact moment, including myself. Not because they didn't understand the value of what they were building. But because they didn't have the right tool to articulate it.
So I built one.
The AI Business Case Builder is a 5-tab Excel workbook that takes you from blank page to exec-ready ROI model in under 30 minutes. Labor savings, error reduction, revenue uplift — all risk-adjusted and confidence-weighted. Three-year model. Auto-generated executive briefing. No finance background required.
It's everything I wish had existed every time I had to justify a technology investment to people that needed numbers, not enthusiasm.
$47. One-time download. Available now on Gumroad — link in the comments.

title_original: AWS Marketplace: Security Review for AI Applications & Services
body_original:
Security Review for AI Applications & Services
Info
Sold by: Deloitte Consulting GmbH
Companies and institutions are increasingly developing or purchasing AI applications & services for enterprise usage. However, one of the main concerns related to this trend is the security of AI and the threats that come with it.
Our security architects can review your AI applications & services and conduct threat modeling for these applications/services and their corresponding cloud environments.
Request private offer
Overview
Pricing
Legal
Resources
Support
Overview
Try agent mode
Create proposal
Ask question
At Deloitte Cyber Security, we offer unique services designed to help enterprise clients assess the security of their AI applications and foster the development of inherently secure AI services. In an era where security threats are continuously evolving, Deloitte stands at the forefront, equipped with the tools, expertise, and strategic thinking necessary to address these challenges head-on.
AI applications, services, and models present their own exclusive set of security threats that require intimate understanding and specialized skills to mitigate. We are well-versed in diverse security threats that AI systems can be prone to, including model inversion attacks, prompt injections, adversarial attacks, poisoning attacks, and extraction attacks. Our services cover generative AI models such as Large Language Models (LLM) or image models where the potential misuse can be significantly high.
Our approach to addressing these issues is systematic and well-structured. It begins with onboarding, during which we familiarize ourselves with our client's specific circumstances and needs. This leads into the information-gathering stage that involves a comprehensive overview of the client's architectural, software, cloud and process infrastructure.
We follow a standardized approach that includes scoping, information gathering, threat analysis, recommending mitigating measures, and reporting. This approach provides you with a comprehensive overview of the overall security aspects of your applications and services.
Following this, we perform an in-depth system architecture review that concentrates on uncovering potential risks. Our specialized security architects have a deep understanding of the interplay between AI and underlying technologies which assists in thorough evaluations.
Our services also include comprehensive threat analysis for recognizing and mitigating potential risks. We help businesses identify vulnerabilities and work together to formulate and implement measures that make their AI portfolio secure, efficient, and resilient to attacks.
Our report consists of a management summary, a system description, data flow diagrams, a list of vulnerabilities along with their severity ratings, a list of countermeasures, and the overall risk level. This will equip you with the necessary information to make informed decisions and implement necessary controls to ensure the security of your AI applications and services.
Creating a safe environment for AI systems is not just about threat mitigation but also involves upholding data privacy and enforcing AI compliance. Our expertise extends to an in-depth understanding of the data privacy aspects of AI applications and associated compliance requirements in cloud environments.
In compliance with national and international standards, we help our clients establish AI compliance in their cloud environment, navigating complexities and ensuring that their AI applications are developed and deployed safely and efficiently.
We embody the principle of 'security by design'. This involves helping our clients develop a robust security concept from the conception of an AI application. We work alongside you from development through to deployment, ensuring that security measures are properly implemented and maintained.
Our service portfolio culminates with a comprehensive report that delivers insights on all aspects of security, from threat analyses and infrastructure vulnerabilities to data privacy considerations and effective mitigation steps.
S Size (Effort = 10 PDs): Scope  well-documented, 3-tier application with minimal complexity and that supports only a single business use case.
Highlights
Our standardized service provides you with rapid, holistic transparency into all security aspects of your AI applications & services, as well as their underlying cloud environments, using a white box approach. This service leverages AI and automation to ensure prompt and efficient delivery. It helps you identify and mitigate potential vulnerabilities swiftly and effectively.
We have hands-on AI security experience working with a range of German companies, from DAX-listed enterprises to medium and small-sized businesses. Our familiarity with local use cases and the specific regulatory requirements of Germany and the EU positions us advantageously to support your needs.
We are equipped to offer a holistic range of services, all from a single source. This includes tool selection, use case development, security governance, cloud compliance automation, penetration test and consultancy services with an emphasis on generative AI.

Deloitte Consulting GmbH

Delivery method
Professional services
Deployed on AWS

title_original: Dr. Christian Essling's Post - LinkedIn
body_original:
I keep hearing „Shouldn’t we go all-in on this one big #AI use case?“ No - and here’s why.
I keep hearing „Shouldn’t we go all-in on this one big #AI use case?“ No - and here’s why.
Most companies don’t need one miracle use case.
They need one hundred small wins that quietly reshape the P&L.
To be clear — a single, massive AI use case certainly can move the needle. But in practice, those lighthouses come with huge trade-offs: they’re expensive, they need #data that often doesn’t exist yet, they require coordination across multiple business units, and they take a long time to deliver results.
Most boards don’t want to wait multiple years for #ROI. And most companies underestimate how much new data they need before a moonshot even becomes feasible.
That’s why - as of right now - the real economic value of AI often comes from the opposite direction:
a collection of smaller, faster wins that stack and compound — driving #productivity across hundreds of processes, not just one.
And if you want to consistently find these high-value opportunities, you need a repeatable way of spotting them. Over the years, I’ve developed a simple shortcut that yielded a broad variety of great projects:
1️⃣ Follow the big budgets.
If a process costs €100m a year, even a 5% improvement is meaningful value creation. And more often than not you don’t even need “AI magic” — proper data integration and combination can create spectacular results.
2️⃣ Look for one-size-fits-all logic.
Wherever everyone (or everything) gets the same marketing message, the same schedule, the same treatment despite that you have more granular data — AI can help to create tailored and personalised experiences that not only improve customer experience but also drive conversion rates. These use cases almost always print money.
3️⃣ Identify unnecessarily manual tasks.
Any process where someone reads something, interprets it, retypes it, routes it, or validates it is a candidate for #automation. LLMs understand the written and spoken word and hence can reduce friction, increase efficiency and reduce errors in manual processes.
So, from what I oberserve, the actual value from AI won‘t come from one single use case but from clever application across the entire value chains - and I‘d like to know if you see this differently.
Would you rather go for the one big use case or for the many smaller ones? Also, what‘s your favorite shortcut to identify high-value use cases in your organization?

I get asked this a lot..."What's the ROI of AWS first party reviews with my listing on AWS Marketplace?" Well, here you go.
PeerSpot is not just another review partner; it is structurally integrated into the AWS Marketplace's core features:
💻 Backend for the "Leave a Review" Button: PeerSpot is the platform that collects the official, first-party reviews when an AWS customer clicks "Leave a review" on a product listing. This confirms its status as the trusted, native data collection engine for AWS Marketplace reviews.
🧠 Powering AI Product Discovery (Agent Mode): The fact that PeerSpot's AWS reviews feed the AI-powered product comparisons, buyer guides, and the new "Agent Mode" means PeerSpot is integral to the future of product discovery on the Marketplace. Agent Mode is AWS's conversational AI interface designed to help buyers find, compare, and select software, making the quality of PeerSpot's verified review data paramount.
🏅 High-Quality, High-Depth Reviews: The conversational, 15+ question approach ensures the reviews are long-form and rich with enterprise-level context (deployment challenges, measurable results, specific use cases), which is highly valuable for both human buyers and the AI systems attempting to parse the data for recommendations.
🎱 Intent Data: PeerSpot also provides intent signals to help sellers target in-market buyers, showing that both platforms offer valuable off-platform data to drive demand into the AWS Marketplace.

Targeting the Enterprise with SaaS or AI offerings?
The Enterprise Software Exec roundtable that I recently participated in - most participants were very bullish on Google vs. competitors to influence AI in their companies at multiple layers in the stack. Why Google?
1️⃣ Leading AI Innovation: embedding advanced, practical AI directly into search and across its entire product ecosystem, leading with Gemini.
2️⃣ Consolidation: They are uniquely positioned to simplify enterprise tech stacks by unifying Cloud, Workspace, and Data into one seamless, AI-integrated platform.
3️⃣ Disruption: Google’s AI is already transforming critical workflows and redefining search, rendering traditional models like SEO obsolete.
What was shared about the competitors relative to Google:
𝐌𝐢𝐜𝐫𝐨𝐬𝐨𝐟𝐭: deep AI integration into enterprise products; sentiment was positive but not as bullish as that of Google's AI approach.
𝐑𝐞𝐝 𝐇𝐚𝐭: infrastructure/Kubernetes, but lacks Google's full-stack capabilities
𝐒𝐞𝐫𝐯𝐢𝐜𝐞𝐍𝐨𝐰: workflow AI, but with a narrower focus than Google
𝐒𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐬𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐜𝐞: despite hype, facing challenges with AI adoption and integration
𝐇𝐮𝐛𝐒𝐩𝐨𝐭: innovative but not at Google's scale
𝐎𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐀𝐈 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐀𝐧𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐨𝐩𝐢𝐜: decentralized platform vs. Google centralizing (not mentioned: Snowflake's announcement WITH Anthropic - a gamechanger).
As companies increase AI investment in 2026, many expect Google to capture greater share and influence how users interact with both consumer and business applications.
What's your take?
How do you see the competitive landscape evolving, and which vendors are best positioned for success?
Although they didn't come up and are in a related space, I think Glean is a dark horse worth watching, too.

title_original: Google’s NotebookLM can sum up your research in a TikTok-style clip
body_original:
Tech
AI
News
Google’s NotebookLM can sum up your research in a TikTok-style clip
The 60-second videos summarize your sources with AI-generated images and narration.
The 60-second videos summarize your sources with AI-generated images and narration.
by Emma Roth
Jun 30, 2026, 7:24 PM UTC
Image: Google
Emma Roth is a news writer who covers the streaming wars, consumer tech, crypto, social media, and much more. Previously, she was a writer and editor at MUO.
Google’s NotebookLM is adding a new way to catch up on your notes: TikTok-style AI videos. The new feature is rolling out to Google AI Ultra and Pro subscribers, allowing NotebookLM to generate 60-second vertical AI clips based on the sources you upload to the app.
The example shared by Google details Australia’s unsuccessful war on emus, pairing paper cutout-style AI art of emus with narration. It adds to some of the other ways NotebookLM lets you interact with your research, including by generating AI podcasts , cinematic videos , and visual explainers.
To generate a 60-second clip, head to NotebookLM on the web or app, select a notebook, and then choose “Video” from the Studio column on the right side of the screen. From there, select “Short,” choose the topic you’d like NotebookLM to focus on (or enter your own), and then hit the “Generate” button.
The feature is rolling out in English only for now, with support for free users coming “soon.”
Follow topics and authors from this story to see more like this in your personalized homepage feed and to receive email updates. Emma Roth

title_original: Google kills Tenor GIF API, forcing changes at X, Discord, and more
body_original:
Skip to content
Text
settings
Story text
Size
Small
Standard
Large
Width
Standard
Wide
Links
Standard
Orange
* Subscribers only
Learn more
Minimize to nav
Google is so famous for killing products that there’s a whole virtual graveyard you can explore. Google’s latest shutdown now has a headstone of its own. Effective today, Google has discontinued the Tenor API, which you may not be familiar with by name. You’ve probably used it, though. Tenor is a database of searchable GIFs, which used to serve animated images to sites like X/Twitter, Discord, and more. Now, it only serves Google—maybe the headstone is a bit premature.
Like many Google products, Tenor started as an independent company. Google came along and bought Tenor in 2018, and it continued running it largely unchanged in the intervening years. Tenor was integrated into Google products like Gboard and Google Messages, but the API also gave other platforms a way to help users find, share, and save GIFs. It’s similar to services like Giphy and Klipy.
In January, Google announced it was going to start winding down that API access. It stopped accepting new integrations at that time, and the end date has now arrived: As of June 30, the Tenor API is no more. Google, a company with nearly 200,000 employees and more than $130 billion in 2025 profit, says it decided to stop supporting the image API so it could better focus its resources. The real problem was probably that Tenor was free, and Google didn’t see a way it could make money from a GIF API.
“As part of an ongoing effort to focus resources on enhancing our core products, we’ve made the decision to sunset the Tenor API on June 30, 2026,” the company explained .
Well, not anymore.
Credit:
Google
Well, not anymore.
Credit:
Google
To be clear, Tenor still exists. You can go to the Tenor website to search for GIFs, and Google’s apps will still source animated images from Tenor (for now). You may not have realized if you were using Tenor through Twitter or Discord, but any favorite GIFs you’ve saved there are gone. Most users didn’t understand easy GIF access was provided by a third-party API and have directed their resulting anger at the platforms themselves.
Websites that relied on Tenor have been shifting to alternatives over the last several weeks, so you can still get your GIF fix without tedious manual uploading. X’s Nikita Bier confirmed on June 20 that the site had migrated away from Tenor. Discord, meanwhile, has tested both Giphy and Klipy, but it looks like most users are seeing Klipy integration now. WhatsApp and Bluesky also appear to be moving to Klipy integration for GIFs.
Users of these websites will see a different selection of GIFs going forward, and not everyone is happy with the selection. Things could improve as more people use Tenor alternatives. Frank Nawabi, who founded Tenor and sold it to Google, is one of the folks behind Klipy. The startup recently raised $3.8 million in funding, and one of the investors was, you guessed it, Google.
Ryan Whitwam
Senior Technology Reporter
Ryan Whitwam
Senior Technology Reporter
Ryan Whitwam is a senior technology reporter at Ars Technica, covering the ways Google, AI, and mobile technology continue to change the world. Over his 20-year career, he's written for Android Police, ExtremeTech, Wirecutter, NY Times, and more. He has reviewed more phones than most people will ever own. You can follow him on Bluesky , where you will see photos of his dozens of mechanical keyboards.

Most users didn’t understand easy GIF access was provided by a third-party API and have directed their resulting anger at the platforms themselves.
Websites that relied on Tenor have been shifting to alternatives over the last several weeks, so you can still get your GIF fix without tedious manual uploading. X’s Nikita Bier confirmed on June 20 that the site had migrated away from Tenor. Discord, meanwhile, has tested both Giphy and Klipy, but it looks like most users are seeing Klipy integration now. WhatsApp and Bluesky also appear to be moving to Klipy integration for GIFs.
Users of these websites will see a different selection of GIFs going forward, and not everyone is happy with the selection. Things could improve as more people use Tenor alternatives. Frank Nawabi, who founded Tenor and sold it to Google, is one of the folks behind Klipy. The startup recently raised $3.8 million in funding, and one of the investors was, you guessed it, Google.
Ryan Whitwam
Senior Technology Reporter
Ryan Whitwam
Senior Technology Reporter
Ryan Whitwam is a senior technology reporter at Ars Technica, covering the ways Google, AI, and mobile technology continue to change the world. Over his 20-year career, he's written for Android Police, ExtremeTech, Wirecutter, NY Times, and more. He has reviewed more phones than most people will ever own. You can follow him on Bluesky , where you will see photos of his dozens of mechanical keyboards.

AI platforms and tools like ChatGPT and Gemini lack the customization , compliance standards, and ability needed to handle your complex, multistep workflows.

But with so many providers available, choosing the right one can be challenging.

1. Neurons Lab: Agentic AI Development for Financial Services
Neurons Lab is a UK and Singapore-based Agentic AI consulting firm serving financial institutions across North America, Europe, and Asia. As an AI enablement partner, we design, build, and implement agentic AI solutions tailored for mid-to-large BFSIs operating in highly regulated environments, including banks, insurers, and wealth management firms. Trusted by 100+ clients, such as HSBC, Visa, and AXA, we co-create agentic systems that run in production and scale across your organization.
As an AWS Advanced Tier partner with competencies in Generative AI (GenAI) and Financial Services, we’ve delivered fully compliant agentic AI projects for leading financial institutions like Visa , AXA, and SMFG .
Neurons Lab’s tech stack aligns with industry regulations and offers enterprise-grade trust and safety features
While generalist agentic AI development providers spread their focus across different industries, we specialize in creating bespoke AI agents and systems for financial services. Combined with our exclusive focus on artificial intelligence, this translates into agentic AI systems capable of automating data-heavy decision-making and complex, multi-step, and compliance-heavy FSI workflows .
Here’s why financial services firms choose Neurons Lab to build AI agents:
Get Efficient, Compliant AI Agents Co-Created with an AI-Exclusive Partner
Plug-and-play AI tools and agent platforms often don’t meet compliance and regulatory checks while strengthening data analysis pipelines. They also fall short in areas like adaptability, customizability, and scalability.
With Neurons Lab, you co-create solutions with us to produce custom AI agents designed to integrate with your existing systems (e.g., CRM, banking core, insurance claims workflows) and scale as you grow. And with enterprise-grade security, governance, and cloud best practices built in from the start, your AI agents will align with your business goals, fit your workflows, and meet regulatory requirements.
For example, a wealth management firm found that off-the-shelf tools like Claude and Perplexity for Finance couldn’t provide the comprehensive, personalized insights or the automation that relationship managers needed to serve clients at scale. We built and deployed an autonomous AI assistant powered by ARKEN , our fully customizable AI accelerator for wealth management .
Using a multi-agent system of autonomous agents , the assistant pulled on client portfolios, live market data, and the firm’s product catalogue to surface actionable, client-specific insights, improving frontline decision-making and the customer experience . As a result, the relationship managers’ capacity increased by 30%.
Neurons Lab’s ARKEN makes use of a multi-agent system to automate a wealth manager’s many repetitive tasks, like generating tailored insights in real time and handling meeting prep, no matter the complexity of their workflows.
Scale Confidently with Full-Service AI Expertise and Support
Moving beyond PoCs to production is difficult without the right expertise. With Neurons Lab, you get end-to-end AI consultancy and hands-on guidance at every stage of your journey, from discovery to AI implementation.
Neurons Lab’s end-to-end AI development and consultancy services
Our 500+ engineers bring deep experience in RAG, orchestration, natural language processing (NLP) and agentic systems backed by proven MLOps and LangOps capabilities to help move you from prototype to production.
For example, in addition to clear roadmaps to keep deployment on track, you’ll have our experts working alongside your AI development teams to build frameworks, transfer knowledge, onboard teams, extend capacity, and scale into more advanced use cases. Once your agents are live, you’ll have our continuous support in monitoring performance, gathering feedback, and making enhancements to ensure post-launch enablement .
Neurons Lab’s four-phase implementation plan across the entire AI project lifecycle
Move from PoC to Production-Ready Agentic Systems in as little as 2 months
Proof of concept (PoC) deployment is often slowed down by system integration complexity and plug-and-play AI agents that aren’t customizable for your specific business and regulatory needs.
With Neurons Lab, you can go from PoC (ready in as little as two weeks) to deployment faster due to our boutique structure that allows us to create AI integration paths and unified data foundations to quickly automate agent workflows. You’ll also have accelerator solutions you can customize to shorten production timelines .
Take our NeuraChat solution as an example of a customer support agent . It combines agentic AI, advanced AI capabilities, emotional intelligence, multimodal context-awareness, and personalized insights to handle everything from routine FAQs to complex, multi-step issues. This drives end-to-end process automation. Because the core components are already in place, you only need to tailor them to your workflows . This means delivery in two to four months.
Lower Costs and Risk with AWS-Backed Funding and Phased Implementation
AI agents may feel out of reach due to your funding constraints. However, as an AWS partner, we extend cloud credits and grants to ease upfront costs and reduce risk. Our phased rollout approach also ensures you validate the return on AI investment early and spread costs over time to avoid large upfront investments.
Who Neurons Lab is Best for
We work best for mid-sized to enterprise financial institutions that consider AI a strategic priority, but struggle to move beyond pilots, integrate AI into their legacy systems, or meet regulatory requirements.
You can trust Neurons Lab to develop AI agents that automate complex and regulated financial services workflows in ways that plug-and-play tools can’t. And as a long-term partner, we help you scale quickly while integrating AI into your operations for lasting success.
However, we understand that we might not be the right fit for every company, so here are four more AI agent development services:

Best for: Global companies and startups seeking fast MVPs and scalable AI-driven software solutions to address their business needs.
How to Choose an AI Agent Development Partner: A Checklist
Picking the right companies developing agentic AI makes the difference between a pilot that stalls and production-grade agents that move KPIs. Firms in highly regulated industries like energy, healthcare, legal, and financial services will also want to ensure that partners know how to connect cleanly to complex systems, meet audit requirements, and show business value fast.
Use the checklist below to qualify providers on the capabilities that matter for secure, compliant, and scalable deployment.
Ask for proof that they can move from pilot to production . Request case studies that show integration with core systems, clear milestones, ROI estimates, and successful audit outcomes (if compliance is a concern).
Ensure deep industry expertise to meet your business processes . Look for hands-on experience in your sector. For example, in financial services, look for experience in KYC/AML, fraud detection, credit risk, regulatory reporting, and model risk management. See how we did this with dAppForge .
See if they have a strong architecture and data foundation . For industries with older systems and homegrown architectures, expect robust pipelines, data lineage, access controls, and cloud architecture that fit legacy environments. See how Neurons Lab built a robust architecture for a leading international asset management firm .
Make sure their security and compliance meet your industry’s regulatory standards (including GDPR where relevant) . Verify their industry-specific certifications, governance processes, audit trails, PII handling, and policy enforcement.
Check for speed with guardrails . For example, prefer phased delivery that lands working systems in weeks, backed by accelerators, like Neurons Lab’s wealth management accelerator , and reference architectures.
Evaluate their know-how in multi-agent orchestration . This includes competency in tool use, function calling, safe interrupt/rollback, AI evaluations , and machine learning monitoring.
Confirm an integration-first approach to connect fragmented systems . Do they have connectors to CRMs, core banking, and claims systems via secure APIs, or event-driven designs and RAG pipelines ?
Qualify their co-creation capabilities for long-term AI enablement . Think joint delivery, documentation, training, and knowledge transfer so teams can operate and extend agents.
Look for clear commercials and support , such as transparent TCO, flexible pricing, IP terms, funding options like cloud credits, SLAs, and post-launch optimization.
The Top AI Agent Development Partners Specialize in Your Industry
The best AI agent development company provides extensive industry and AI expertise and proven AI technologies , ensuring agentic systems that are secure, compliant, and built to scale. The right provider acts as a strategic enablement partner, guiding you from use case design to full-scale deployment and beyond.
Ready to co-develop custom AI agents that deliver measurable business results for your financial services firm? Get in touch with us today .
FAQs on AI Agent Development Companies
Can AI Agents Integrate with my Existing Systems?
Yes, integration is a core part of what AI agents do, since they need access to your data to your systems to automate workflows or provide a foundation for a knowledge graph. In financial services, this could be CRMs and product catalogues or core banking platforms such as Avaloq and Temenos to automate complex workflows that also adhere to regulatory standards.
How smoothly this happens depends on the systems you’re running and the partner you choose to work with, including how they version, evaluate, and deploy AI models.
Which Companies Offer Custom Development Services with Agentic AI for Financial Services Firms?
Many AI consulting companies, from large consultancies like Infosys to specialized boutique firms like Neurons Lab, offer custom development services for agentic AI for financial services . Other companies include Emerline, Openkit, Codebrew, and Hatchworks AI.
How do I Choose the Right AI Agent Development Partner for my Financial Services Firm?
To choose the right AI agent development partner, look for a company with strong technical and industry expertise and a track record of delivering successful AI agent programs in the financial services industry. They should know how to address integration complexity, fragmented data sources, and regulatory requirements. Also, consider how quickly they deliver projects and whether they provide ongoing support.

Yes, integration is a core part of what AI agents do, since they need access to your data to your systems to automate workflows or provide a foundation for a knowledge graph. In financial services, this could be CRMs and product catalogues or core banking platforms such as Avaloq and Temenos to automate complex workflows that also adhere to regulatory standards.
How smoothly this happens depends on the systems you’re running and the partner you choose to work with, including how they version, evaluate, and deploy AI models.
Which Companies Offer Custom Development Services with Agentic AI for Financial Services Firms?
Many AI consulting companies, from large consultancies like Infosys to specialized boutique firms like Neurons Lab, offer custom development services for agentic AI for financial services . Other companies include Emerline, Openkit, Codebrew, and Hatchworks AI.
How do I Choose the Right AI Agent Development Partner for my Financial Services Firm?
To choose the right AI agent development partner, look for a company with strong technical and industry expertise and a track record of delivering successful AI agent programs in the financial services industry. They should know how to address integration complexity, fragmented data sources, and regulatory requirements. Also, consider how quickly they deliver projects and whether they provide ongoing support.

title_original: Browser Use: Funding, Team & Investors - Startup Intros
body_original:
# Browser Use: The Rapid Rise of AI-Powered Web Automation
High-Level Overview
Browser Use is an open-source web agent project that enables artificial intelligence systems to control and interact with web browsers autonomously[1]. Founded by Gregor and Magnus, the project has achieved remarkable momentum—accumulating 50,000 GitHub stars within just three months of launch, establishing itself as one of the fastest-growing open-source AI projects[2]. The platform solves a fundamental problem: prior to Browser Use, there was no practical interface allowing large language models to navigate the web like humans do.
The company serves developers and enterprises seeking to automate web-based workflows, from login automation and data extraction to quality assurance testing and CRM integrations[2]. Browser Use operates on a dual-revenue model: the open-source core remains free under an MIT license, while a commercial hosted cloud service (Browser Use Cloud) launched recently at $30 per month—significantly undercutting competitors like OpenAI's Operator[1]. With over 15,000 active developers and a Discord community of 5,000 members, Browser Use has rapidly become the de facto standard for AI-driven browser automation.
Origin Story
The project emerged from a simple weekend experiment when founders Gregor and Magnus asked themselves: "How hard could it be to build the interface between LLMs and the web?"[1] The answer proved both challenging and valuable. They built an initial prototype in just four days and launched it on Hacker News, where the response was immediate and overwhelming—validating their core thesis that AI-driven web automation represented a genuine market need[2].
This rapid validation led to explosive growth. Within three months, the project accumulated 50,000 GitHub stars and attracted 15,000+ developers actively using and contributing to the codebase[2]. The timing proved critical: just as the founders were scaling their open-source offering, OpenAI launched Operator, which immediately generated demand from their community for a hosted version of Browser Use. The team responded by shipping Browser Use Cloud within days, demonstrating both agility and market awareness[1].
Core Differentiators
Open-Source Flexibility with Commercial Optionality
Browser Use's architecture allows developers to choose any LLM—Gemini, Sonnet, Qwen, or even DeepSeek-R1—rather than locking users into a single model ecosystem[1]. This flexibility has become a significant competitive advantage as the LLM landscape fragments across multiple capable providers.
Hosted Cloud Service with Enterprise Features
The Browser Use Cloud offering addresses real operational challenges that plague browser automation: proxy rotation, persistent session management (requiring login only once), and parallel instance scaling[1]. Priced at $30 monthly, it undercuts Operator while providing immediate availability without waiting for competitor API releases[1].
Vibrant Developer Ecosystem
The project has spawned a curated ecosystem of downstream projects built on Browser Use, including SDET-GENIE (AI-powered QA automation), SpiderCreator (web scraping), and Rebrowse (screen-recording to executable workflows)[3]. This network effect strengthens the platform's moat and creates switching costs for developers who have built integrations.
Rapid Feature Development
The team is actively shipping new capabilities: follow-up questions, task reruns, voice mode, and scheduled agent starts represent a product roadmap that extends well beyond the initial MVP[1].
Role in the Broader Tech Landscape
Browser Use sits at the intersection of three powerful trends: the maturation of large language models capable of reasoning about visual interfaces, the explosion of demand for AI-driven automation, and the open-source community's hunger for alternatives to closed commercial platforms.
The timing is particularly significant. As enterprises grapple with legacy system integration and repetitive knowledge-work automation, Browser Use offers a low-friction entry point—developers can prototype solutions in hours rather than months. The project also represents a broader shift in how AI systems interact with human-designed interfaces: rather than requiring APIs or custom integrations, agents can now navigate the web as humans do, dramatically expanding the scope of automatable workflows.
Browser Use's influence extends beyond its direct user base. By establishing an open standard for browser automation, the project has effectively set the baseline for what competitors must match. OpenAI's Operator launch within months of Browser Use's growth suggests the project has validated and accelerated market awareness of this category. The founders' decision to price their commercial offering significantly below Operator signals confidence in their product differentiation and willingness to compete on value rather than brand alone.
Quick Take & Future Outlook
Browser Use has achieved something rare: genuine product-market fit validated through organic community adoption rather than marketing spend. The 50,000 GitHub stars in three months reflects authentic developer demand, not hype.
The company's trajectory suggests several likely developments. First, the API release (announced as imminent) will unlock enterprise adoption by enabling direct integration into existing software platforms—a critical milestone for moving beyond individual developer use cases. Second, the ecosystem of downstream projects will likely accelerate, creating network effects that make Browser Use the default choice for AI-powered browser automation. Third, the founders' openness to custom integrations signals willingness to pursue high-touch enterprise deals alongside self-serve cloud offerings.
The broader question is whether Browser Use can maintain its momentum as the category matures and larger players invest heavily. History suggests that open-source projects with strong community moats and clear commercial models often outcompete closed alternatives—particularly when they offer genuine flexibility and lower costs. Browser Use's founders appear to understand this dynamic, positioning the open-source core as the community anchor while the cloud service captures commercial value.
The vision—"tell your computer what to do, and it gets it done"—represents a fundamental shift in how humans interact with digital systems. If Browser Use can execute on that vision while maintaining developer trust, the project could become infrastructure for the AI era, much as Kubernetes became infrastructure for containerization.

Stop shouting at your LLMs. 🗣️ It isn’t a failure of intelligence; it’s a failure of Steering.
Most people try to control AI with "willpower prompting"—using all-caps and begging the model to stay on topic. In the Navigator’s Lab, we call this a reactive strategy. And in 2026, it’s a strategy that doesn't scale.
Chapter 7 of The AI Navigator is live today: Steering Behavior.
We’re moving from the "polite request" of a prompt to the "enforced boundary" of a Safeguard Layer.
Here is why your instructions are drifting:
📍 The Context Paradox: As context windows grow to 2M+ tokens, your rules get "diluted." Research from the giants like OpenAI and Anthropic shows that instruction adherence degrades as data density increases.
📍 Internal vs. External: A prompt is internal conditioning. A Safeguard is external enforcement. We’re deep-diving into how to use NVIDIA NeMo-Guardrails and Llama Guard to build "Armor" around your logic.
📍 The Individual ROI: This isn't just for devs. If you're using ChatGPT or Copilot, you are the Architect of your own focus. We explore how "Soft Safeguards" reclaim 3+ hours of "babysitting" time per week. ⏳
🔗 Read the full Chapter 7 here: https://lnkd.in/ewi-rSig
🚀 What’s coming next:
🛠️ Friday: I’m dropping the Chapter 7 Lab details—the technical setup for LangGraph state machines and Garak red-teaming.
📱 Early Next Week: Chapter 8: Efficiency & The Edge. How to shrink these guarded systems to run locally on your own hardware.
Points to think about:
💭 Is your AI a "chat partner" or a "verified component"?
💭 Are you paying the "Hallucination Tax" in manual review time?
If you’re building internal tools and want to see the logic circuit before Friday's post, drop me a DM! 📥

At what point are you comfortable with deploying an AI workflow to production?
This is a question that every tech leader needs to be able to answer, but it's not an easy one. For starters, ask yourself these questions:
- How consistent are my outputs (i.e. if the workflow runs multiple times, do the outputs change)? Are any inconsistencies within appropriate bounds?
- Does this workflow consume an appropriate amount of resources (tokens, GPU, wall clock time, etc)?
- Do I have error handling for all edge cases (invalid input, API errors, heavy load, etc)?
- Are there validation mechanisms on your inputs and outputs? How do you know that you aren't at risk for prompt injection, exposure of PII, or just delivering a bad user experience?
If you can't answer one or more of these questions, then the chances are pretty good that your workflow is not ready to ship.
Did I miss anything?

P95 latency shows the real user experience, not just the average.
While average response time may look good, a small percentage of slow requests can significantly impact users. P95 helps capture those delays and highlights performance bottlenecks in the system.
In AI and distributed systems, optimizing for P95 ensures consistency, reliability, and better user satisfaction.

Released glm5.1-distill on Hugging Face .
It's a 1.2B parameter chat model built on Liquid AI LiquidAI/LFM2.5-1.2B-Base, supervised-fine-tuned on 50k conversations from Jackrong/GLM-5.1-Reasoning-1M-Cleaned. The goal was to bring some of the conversational reasoning behavior of larger Z.ai GLM-5.1 teacher models into a small, edge-friendly student that fits on a single consumer GPU.
Training setup:
- Method: LoRA SFT via Unsloth AI , then merged back to a single 16-bit checkpoint
- Rank 16, alpha 16, train_on_responses_only
- 1 epoch, effective batch size 32, packed at seq length 2048
- AdamW, lr 2e-5 linear with 50 warmup steps, BF16
- Single GPU, fits comfortably on a free Colab T4
The LFM2 hybrid architecture (short conv blocks plus grouped query attention) keeps inference fast and memory low, which is why I picked it as a base instead of a standard transformer.
This is a starting checkpoint, not a finished assistant. It needs evaluation on real benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, IFEval, MT-Bench) and probably another fine-tuning pass for any specific domain. Treat it as a base to build on.
Model: https://lnkd.in/d3TCk936
Pau Labarta Bajo Carol Lin Michael Han (Unsloth) Daniel Han

AMD's AI Director just dropped the receipts on Claude Code.
Stella Laurenzo analyzed 6,852 sessions, tracked 2,34,760 tool calls, and reached one conclusion:
"Claude Code cannot be trusted for complex engineering tasks."
Here's what the data showed:
- Thinking depth dropped 67%
- File reading collapsed from 6.6x to 2x
- 1 in 3 edits were made blind - without reading the file
- Multi-agent workflows producing 191K lines/weekend? Completely broken
- AMD's team has already switched providers
Anthropic's fix? Type `/effort max` and set an env variable.
That's like downgrading your car engine and telling you to press the gas harder.
The real lesson here - don't blindly trust any AI tool. Monitor it. Measure it. Always have a backup plan.
---
Save this if you use AI coding tools daily. 🔖
Share this with a dev friend who uses Claude Code.

Your LLMs are silently degrading, and your APM won't catch it. Welcome to "AI Shrinkflation."
Providers are continuously tweaking reasoning depths, safety layers, and routing pools to manage their own compute costs. The API still returns a 200 OK, but behind the scenes, your workload is secretly degrading.
Case in point: A recent analysis of 234,000+ tool calls showed a sudden 67% drop in an LLM's thinking depth. The result? A 122x cost multiplier due to agent thrashing, retries, and premature stops.
> Better prompts won't fix this. You need to architect for drift:
- Stop direct SDK calls: Build strict abstraction layers so you can swap models instantly.
- Golden Evals: Run representative workflows daily to catch behavioral degradation before users do.
- Harness Guards: Enforce read-before-edit rules and strict cost-per-task alerts to catch spinning agents.
Treat LLMs as swappable, volatile dependencies, not permanent fixtures.
I broke down the metrics, the AMD case study, and the exact architectural patterns you need to protect your systems in my latest post.
Read the full deep dive here: https://lnkd.in/dBB5guBh

5 of 7 manufacturing apis failed the agent test.
not because the endpoints were broken.
because i couldn't get to the docs.
last week i scored the seven biggest manufacturing APIs – xometry, protolabs, pcbway, jlcpcb, shapeways, hubs, fictiv – across eight agent-readiness dimensions. instant quote. structured pricing. dfm feedback. order status. auth. rate limits. the basics.
headline number: shapeways 63 out of 100. that's the current ceiling.
the deeper finding: 5 of 7 public api pages were gated, 404, or auth-walled before i could read them.
an ai agent trying to place an order today doesn't hit a pricing problem.
it hits a paywall on the documentation.
this is the structural barrier nobody talks about.
every conference deck on "ai in manufacturing" assumes the apis are ready. they aren't.
you cannot fork what you cannot reach.
you cannot automate what you cannot crawl.
you cannot build an agent on top of a pdf that lives behind a login form.
the fix isn't smarter agents.
it's manufacturers treating their developer docs like the product they already are.
the forkable factory starts the day a curl command returns json.
where does your stack break first when you try to automate a supplier?
#manufacturing #ai

Recently, I’ve been using OpenClaw to gradually delegate repetitive and time-consuming tasks to AI.
As I automated tasks one by one, I realized that many workflows that previously took 1–2 hours can now be handled effectively by today’s AI.
By automating tasks such as news aggregation, scheduling, and Excel data management, I’ve been able to free up roughly 3 hours per day.
Initially, I experimented with subscription-based APIs, but with a usage of 1–2 million tokens per day, the cost quickly became unsustainable. $20 a day, no joke. Unfortunately, I don't have that kind of money.
This led me to shift toward a local setup. Considering power consumption and noise constraints, I chose a DGX Spark-based environment. I don't want to give my colleagues the gift of hot wind and noise.
And I ultimately adopted the MSI EdgeExpert, which uses the same nvidia's gb10 chipset but offers better thermal control and a significantly lower price point. I mean cheaper, that's the point. I am not a rich person who spends an extra $1.5k to increase it by 3TB. And the EdgeExpert 1TB is also $3,000. If I hadn't make money from stocks, I wouldn't even dare to dream of that price. Thank you, Samsung and SK Hynix.
Put the chatter aside, After purchasing the PC and setting up OpenClaw, I installed GPT OSS 120B. I chose this model because I don't mind if the response is a bit slow, but I want to be less dumb.
While running GPT OSS 120B locally has some performance drawbacks compared to commercial models, a major advantage is that it can be operated without ongoing costs.
Additionally, utilizing CUDA was beneficial because it enabled large-scale data preprocessing, particularly through tools like cuDF. Since I also handle tasks analyzing reusable cup and bowl's status data at work, processing tens of millions of rows of data accumulated over four years used to take far too long with conventional CPU computations.
I’m still in the learning phase, but I believe AI-driven workflow automation has the potential to create a shift comparable to the introduction of Excel.
I will share content related to Open Claw and AX progress whenever I remember.

From LLM 'Brain' 🧠 to the 'Nervous System'
In the shift from chatbots to autonomous agents - one major hurdle is Resource Management. If you run several agents at once, they are likely to lose memory, fight for tokens, and crash the context window.
The paper AIOS: LLM Agent Operating System: https://lnkd.in/gejFFX2S offers a brilliant fix. It treats LLM like a CPU and builds an AI Kernel around it. I found this quite insightful as it applies classic OS logic (scheduling, memory management, and storage) to AI. The AIOS Kernel:
✅ Schedules tasks so no single agent hogs the LLM
✅ Context-switches smoothly, preserving 'thoughts' without wasting tokens
✅ Secures the boundaries between different AI tools
Architecture like AIOS is what will make autonomous agents highly reliable.
If you’re interested in the plumbing that will power AGI, this is a must-read.

At what point are you comfortable with deploying an AI workflow to production?
This is a question that every tech leader needs to be able to answer, but it's not an easy one. For starters, ask yourself these questions:
- How consistent are my outputs (i.e. if the workflow runs multiple times, do the outputs change)? Are any inconsistencies within appropriate bounds?
- Does this workflow consume an appropriate amount of resources (tokens, GPU, wall clock time, etc)?
- Do I have error handling for all edge cases (invalid input, API errors, heavy load, etc)?
- Are there validation mechanisms on your inputs and outputs? How do you know that you aren't at risk for prompt injection, exposure of PII, or just delivering a bad user experience?
If you can't answer one or more of these questions, then the chances are pretty good that your workflow is not ready to ship.
Did I miss anything?

P95 latency shows the real user experience, not just the average.
While average response time may look good, a small percentage of slow requests can significantly impact users. P95 helps capture those delays and highlights performance bottlenecks in the system.
In AI and distributed systems, optimizing for P95 ensures consistency, reliability, and better user satisfaction.

Released glm5.1-distill on Hugging Face .
It's a 1.2B parameter chat model built on Liquid AI LiquidAI/LFM2.5-1.2B-Base, supervised-fine-tuned on 50k conversations from Jackrong/GLM-5.1-Reasoning-1M-Cleaned. The goal was to bring some of the conversational reasoning behavior of larger Z.ai GLM-5.1 teacher models into a small, edge-friendly student that fits on a single consumer GPU.
Training setup:
- Method: LoRA SFT via Unsloth AI , then merged back to a single 16-bit checkpoint
- Rank 16, alpha 16, train_on_responses_only
- 1 epoch, effective batch size 32, packed at seq length 2048
- AdamW, lr 2e-5 linear with 50 warmup steps, BF16
- Single GPU, fits comfortably on a free Colab T4
The LFM2 hybrid architecture (short conv blocks plus grouped query attention) keeps inference fast and memory low, which is why I picked it as a base instead of a standard transformer.
This is a starting checkpoint, not a finished assistant. It needs evaluation on real benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, IFEval, MT-Bench) and probably another fine-tuning pass for any specific domain. Treat it as a base to build on.
Model: https://lnkd.in/d3TCk936
Pau Labarta Bajo Carol Lin Michael Han (Unsloth) Daniel Han

AMD's AI Director just dropped the receipts on Claude Code.
Stella Laurenzo analyzed 6,852 sessions, tracked 2,34,760 tool calls, and reached one conclusion:
"Claude Code cannot be trusted for complex engineering tasks."
Here's what the data showed:
- Thinking depth dropped 67%
- File reading collapsed from 6.6x to 2x
- 1 in 3 edits were made blind - without reading the file
- Multi-agent workflows producing 191K lines/weekend? Completely broken
- AMD's team has already switched providers
Anthropic's fix? Type `/effort max` and set an env variable.
That's like downgrading your car engine and telling you to press the gas harder.
The real lesson here - don't blindly trust any AI tool. Monitor it. Measure it. Always have a backup plan.

Your LLMs are silently degrading, and your APM won't catch it. Welcome to "AI Shrinkflation."
Providers are continuously tweaking reasoning depths, safety layers, and routing pools to manage their own compute costs. The API still returns a 200 OK, but behind the scenes, your workload is secretly degrading.
Case in point: A recent analysis of 234,000+ tool calls showed a sudden 67% drop in an LLM's thinking depth. The result? A 122x cost multiplier due to agent thrashing, retries, and premature stops.
> Better prompts won't fix this. You need to architect for drift:
- Stop direct SDK calls: Build strict abstraction layers so you can swap models instantly.
- Golden Evals: Run representative workflows daily to catch behavioral degradation before users do.
- Harness Guards: Enforce read-before-edit rules and strict cost-per-task alerts to catch spinning agents.
Treat LLMs as swappable, volatile dependencies, not permanent fixtures.
I broke down the metrics, the AMD case study, and the exact architectural patterns you need to protect your systems in my latest post.
Read the full deep dive here: https://lnkd.in/dBB5guBh
#ai #development #engineering

5 of 7 manufacturing apis failed the agent test.
not because the endpoints were broken.
because i couldn't get to the docs.
last week i scored the seven biggest manufacturing APIs – xometry, protolabs, pcbway, jlcpcb, shapeways, hubs, fictiv – across eight agent-readiness dimensions. instant quote. structured pricing. dfm feedback. order status. auth. rate limits. the basics.
headline number: shapeways 63 out of 100. that's the current ceiling.
the deeper finding: 5 of 7 public api pages were gated, 404, or auth-walled before i could read them.
an ai agent trying to place an order today doesn't hit a pricing problem.
it hits a paywall on the documentation.
this is the structural barrier nobody talks about.
every conference deck on "ai in manufacturing" assumes the apis are ready. they aren't.
you cannot fork what you cannot reach.
you cannot automate what you cannot crawl.
you cannot build an agent on top of a pdf that lives behind a login form.
the fix isn't smarter agents.
it's manufacturers treating their developer docs like the product they already are.
the forkable factory starts the day a curl command returns json.
where does your stack break first when you try to automate a supplier?
#manufacturing #ai

Recently, I’ve been using OpenClaw to gradually delegate repetitive and time-consuming tasks to AI.
As I automated tasks one by one, I realized that many workflows that previously took 1–2 hours can now be handled effectively by today’s AI.
By automating tasks such as news aggregation, scheduling, and Excel data management, I’ve been able to free up roughly 3 hours per day.
Initially, I experimented with subscription-based APIs, but with a usage of 1–2 million tokens per day, the cost quickly became unsustainable. $20 a day, no joke. Unfortunately, I don't have that kind of money.
This led me to shift toward a local setup. Considering power consumption and noise constraints, I chose a DGX Spark-based environment. I don't want to give my colleagues the gift of hot wind and noise.
And I ultimately adopted the MSI EdgeExpert, which uses the same nvidia's gb10 chipset but offers better thermal control and a significantly lower price point. I mean cheaper, that's the point. I am not a rich person who spends an extra $1.5k to increase it by 3TB. And the EdgeExpert 1TB is also $3,000. If I hadn't make money from stocks, I wouldn't even dare to dream of that price. Thank you, Samsung and SK Hynix.
Put the chatter aside, After purchasing the PC and setting up OpenClaw, I installed GPT OSS 120B. I chose this model because I don't mind if the response is a bit slow, but I want to be less dumb.
While running GPT OSS 120B locally has some performance drawbacks compared to commercial models, a major advantage is that it can be operated without ongoing costs.
Additionally, utilizing CUDA was beneficial because it enabled large-scale data preprocessing, particularly through tools like cuDF. Since I also handle tasks analyzing reusable cup and bowl's status data at work, processing tens of millions of rows of data accumulated over four years used to take far too long with conventional CPU computations.
I’m still in the learning phase, but I believe AI-driven workflow automation has the potential to create a shift comparable to the introduction of Excel.
I will share content related to Open Claw and AX progress whenever I remember.

What remains open is whether this preview turns into a durable market shift or stays a strong but transitional step.

From LLM 'Brain' 🧠 to the 'Nervous System'
In the shift from chatbots to autonomous agents - one major hurdle is Resource Management. If you run several agents at once, they are likely to lose memory, fight for tokens, and crash the context window.
The paper AIOS: LLM Agent Operating System: https://lnkd.in/gejFFX2S offers a brilliant fix. It treats LLM like a CPU and builds an AI Kernel around it. I found this quite insightful as it applies classic OS logic (scheduling, memory management, and storage) to AI. The AIOS Kernel:
✅ Schedules tasks so no single agent hogs the LLM
✅ Context-switches smoothly, preserving 'thoughts' without wasting tokens
✅ Secures the boundaries between different AI tools
Architecture like AIOS is what will make autonomous agents highly reliable.
If you’re interested in the plumbing that will power AGI, this is a must-read.
#AI #LLM #TechInnovation #SoftwareArchitecture #AutonomousAgents

AI ROI Dataset: 200 B2B Deployments Analysis (2022-2025)
Dataset Description
Empirical analysis of 200 artificial intelligence (AI) deployments in French B2B companies (2022-2025). Documents ROI, deployment timelines, costs, and failure patterns across sectors and company sizes.
Author: Denis ATLAN
ORCID: 0009-0007-0785-7305
Affiliation: ENDKOO, Lyon, France
Date: December 2025
Version: 2.0
Key Statistics
Success Rate: 82.5% (vs. 5-20% market benchmark)
Breakeven Period: 8 months median
Top Sectors: Retail (+242% ROI), Finance (+187%), Manufacturing (+171%)
Human-in-the-Loop Adoption: 88.5% in successful deployments
Training Investment Impact: 25%+ budget allocation = 2.4× ROI multiplier
⚡ Impact & Performance
Median ROI: +159.8% (over 24-month horizon)
Payback Period: < 6 months
Methodology: Based on audited median results (Expert IA Lyon)
Research Contribution
This dataset challenges three conventional assumptions:
Budget Size Paradox: Small projects (<€15K) achieve 2.1× higher ROI than large projects (>€100K)
Human-in-the-Loop Superiority: Governance models requiring human validation reduce critical incidents by 4.3× while achieving higher ROI
Training as ROI Multiplier: Companies investing 25%+ of AI budget in training achieve 2.4× higher ROI
Dataset Structure
Total Records: 200
Total Variables: 20
Format: CSV
Size: ~50 KB

Data Collection Methodology
Research Design: Longitudinal observational study (2022-2025)
Sample Frame: B2B companies in Rhône-Alpes region (France) that engaged consulting services for AI deployment
Inclusion Criteria:
Minimum 10 employees
AI project budget > €4,000
Anonymized financial data sharing agreement
Minimum 6-month post-deployment observation
Data Sources:
CRM/ERP exports (budget, time logs, licenses, timelines)
Quarterly structured interviews (adoption rates, satisfaction, challenges)
Financial audits (30% sample with third-party validation)
Project management tools (completion rates, incidents)
Quality Controls:
Outlier detection: ROI > 1000% removed (n=12, 6%)
Survivorship bias mitigation: Failed projects actively documented (n=23, 11.5%)
Self-reporting bias correction: 30% cross-validated with direct financial data (discrepancy rate: 8%)
Ethical Compliance:
GDPR compliant: All data anonymized, no PII retained
Informed consent: Written authorization from all participants
Data security: Encrypted storage, restricted access
Use Cases
This dataset enables:
ROI Benchmarking: Compare AI project performance against validated empirical data
Success Factor Analysis: Identify predictors of project success/failure
Budget Optimization: Understand relationship between investment size and returns
Risk Assessment: Analyze failure patterns and mitigation strategies
Technology Comparison: Evaluate different AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Custom ML)
Training Impact Study: Quantify relationship between training investment and outcomes
Academic Research: Empirical foundation for AI adoption studies

Limitations
Geographic: 100% French companies (Rhône-Alpes region)
Selection Bias: Companies that hired external consultant may differ from self-implementers
Time Horizon: Most projects tracked <18 months (long-term sustainability not captured)
Attribution Challenge: Isolating AI contribution from concurrent organizational changes

AI ROI Dataset: 200 B2B Deployments Analysis (2022-2025)
Dataset Description
Empirical analysis of 200 artificial intelligence (AI) deployments in French B2B companies (2022-2025). Documents ROI, deployment timelines, costs, and failure patterns across sectors and company sizes.
Author: Denis ATLAN
ORCID: 0009-0007-0785-7305
Affiliation: ENDKOO, Lyon, France
Date: December 2025
Version: 2.0
Key Statistics
Success Rate: 82.5% (vs. 5-20% market benchmark)
Breakeven Period: 8 months median
Top Sectors: Retail (+242% ROI), Finance (+187%), Manufacturing (+171%)
Human-in-the-Loop Adoption: 88.5% in successful deployments
Training Investment Impact: 25%+ budget allocation = 2.4× ROI multiplier
⚡ Impact & Performance
Median ROI: +159.8% (over 24-month horizon)
Payback Period: < 6 months
Methodology: Based on audited median results (Expert IA Lyon)
Research Contribution
This dataset challenges three conventional assumptions:
Budget Size Paradox: Small projects (<€15K) achieve 2.1× higher ROI than large projects (>€100K)
Human-in-the-Loop Superiority: Governance models requiring human validation reduce critical incidents by 4.3× while achieving higher ROI
Training as ROI Multiplier: Companies investing 25%+ of AI budget in training achieve 2.4× higher ROI
Dataset Structure
Total Records: 200
Total Variables: 20
Format: CSV
Size: ~50 KB
Variables
Column
Type
Description
Example Values

string
Unique identifier
P001-P200
year
int
Deployment year

string

ChatGPT API, ChatGPT Enterprise, Claude Pro, Claude API, Custom ML, Gemini, Mistral, Open-source

Diagnostic phase duration (days)
7-45

14-90

0 (success), 1 (failure)
failure_reason

Wrong Use Case, No Executive Sponsor, Tool-First Thinking, No Change Management, Unrealistic Timeline, NULL (if success)
human_in_loop
int
HITL governance (0/1)
0 (no HITL), 1 (HITL implemented)
Data Collection Methodology
Research Design: Longitudinal observational study (2022-2025)
Sample Frame: B2B companies in Rhône-Alpes region (France) that engaged consulting services for AI deployment
Inclusion Criteria:
Minimum 10 employees
AI project budget > €4,000
Anonymized financial data sharing agreement
Minimum 6-month post-deployment observation
Data Sources:
CRM/ERP exports (budget, time logs, licenses, timelines)
Quarterly structured interviews (adoption rates, satisfaction, challenges)
Financial audits (30% sample with third-party validation)
Project management tools (completion rates, incidents)
Quality Controls:
Outlier detection: ROI > 1000% removed (n=12, 6%)
Survivorship bias mitigation: Failed projects actively documented (n=23, 11.5%)
Self-reporting bias correction: 30% cross-validated with direct financial data (discrepancy rate: 8%)
Ethical Compliance:
GDPR compliant: All data anonymized, no PII retained
Informed consent: Written authorization from all participants
Data security: Encrypted storage, restricted access
Use Cases
This dataset enables:
ROI Benchmarking: Compare AI project performance against validated empirical data
Success Factor Analysis: Identify predictors of project success/failure
Budget Optimization: Understand relationship between investment size and returns
Risk Assessment: Analyze failure patterns and mitigation strategies
Technology Comparison: Evaluate different AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Custom ML)
Training Impact Study: Quantify relationship between training investment and outcomes
Academic Research: Empirical foundation for AI adoption studies

Limitations
Geographic: 100% French companies (Rhône-Alpes region)
Selection Bias: Companies that hired external consultant may differ from self-implementers
Time Horizon: Most projects tracked <18 months (long-term sustainability not captured)
Attribution Challenge: Isolating AI contribution from concurrent organizational changes

title_original: AI Workflow Automation ROI | How to Calculate & Maximize ... - NiCE

AI Workflow Automation ROI
How to Build a Business Case That Gets Approved
View Demo Request a Quote
On this page Cost side of ROI equation

Frequently asked questions
Cost side of ROI equation
Benefit side of ROI equation
Strategic value over time
ROI benchmarks by use case
Frequently asked questions
AI workflow automation ROI is one of the most compelling business cases in enterprise technology — when calculated rigorously. According to NiCE customer data, median three-year ROI is 220%, with contact center use cases achieving payback in 6–9 months and enterprise back-office use cases in 9–18 months. The key to a credible business case is building a structured model that connects specific automation investments to specific, measurable business outcomes.
Cost Side of the ROI Equation
Include all cost categories: platform licensing, professional services and implementation, integration development (often underestimated by 30–50%), change management and training, ongoing model maintenance, and internal team time for continuous optimization. A realistic cost model — even a conservative one — builds more credibility than an optimistic one that creates problems when actuals come in higher.
Two Leaders.
One platform.
At NiCE, we’re setting the standard for AI-first customer experience.
Get the reports
Benefit Side of the ROI Equation
Primary benefits to quantify: Labor capacity reclaimed (hours saved × fully-loaded cost × annualized volume), Error reduction (rework cost × error rate reduction), Throughput expansion (value of additional volume handled without headcount), and Quality improvement (revenue impact of CSAT gains and churn reduction). NiCE customers consistently report 30–70% cost-per-transaction reductions as the primary hard-dollar benefit.
Strategic Value: The Benefits That Compound Over Time
Include strategic value in your model: the competitive advantage of 2–3x faster process cycles, talent retention improvement (15–20% engagement increase for AI-augmented teams), and platform optionality (the ability to add new use cases without proportional investment increases). These benefits are real and measurable — they belong in the ROI case, not just the qualitative summary.
ROI Benchmarks by Use Case
Contact center AI workflow automation: 6–9 month payback, 150–250% 3-year ROI. Enterprise customer service: 9–12 month payback, 200–300% 3-year ROI. Finance and AP automation: 9–18 month payback, 180–280% 3-year ROI. HR and onboarding automation: 12–18 month payback, 150–220% 3-year ROI. (Source: NiCE customer deployment benchmarks)

FAQs
What is a realistic ROI timeframe for AI workflow automation? NiCE customer data shows contact center use cases achieve payback in 6–9 months, enterprise customer service in 9–12 months, and back-office automation in 9–18 months. Median three-year ROI across deployment types is 220%, driven primarily by 30–70% cost-per-transaction reductions and measurable throughput expansion.
What costs are most commonly underestimated in AI workflow automation ROI models? Integration development is underestimated by 30–50% in most ROI models. Change management and training, ongoing model maintenance, and internal analyst time for continuous optimization are also frequently excluded. A conservative, comprehensive cost model builds credibility — budget holders will stress-test your assumptions, and realistic numbers hold up better than optimistic ones.

title_original: AI ROI: Automate Customer Value Not Internal Productivity - LinkedIn
body_original:
I have been saying that 𝘁𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻𝗺𝗮𝘅𝘅𝗶𝗻𝗴 is a self-serving trend manufactured by the frontier labs that will soon meet the reality of enterprise leaders needing to show fast ROI.
I have been saying that 𝘁𝗼𝗸𝗲𝗻𝗺𝗮𝘅𝘅𝗶𝗻𝗴 is a self-serving trend manufactured by the frontier labs that will soon meet the reality of enterprise leaders needing to show fast ROI. Great to see Axios reporting today how that has started:
𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗰𝗮𝘀𝗲𝘀: "Most people default to automating tasks they dislike rather than tasks most valuable to the company. Instead, they should focus on using AI to drive revenue." Revenue comes from expanding at existing customers and acquiring new customers. This means let's automate the workflows that wow customers ahead of internal productivity.
𝗖𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀: "Employees were using AI models to check the weather. That gets expensive fast: Enterprise AI plans are not truly all-you-can-eat, and even simple chatbot queries can carry heavy token costs." Measure outcomes and not just tokens so that you understand the ROI equation fast.
𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝘀: "We are the bottleneck to more efficient adoption, as we're still catching up on AI. Leadership isn't always helping: Throwing AI licenses at the wall and seeing what sticks aka thousand-flowers-bloom approach isn't leading to tangible returns." AI coding agents continuously being prompted by developers on their local machine is not the same as always-on, long-running, background agents that correlate across 100s of live data sources. When such agents achieve outcomes without tokenmaxxing, we remove the human bottleneck.
